ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Assam Chicken; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' class. A more specific term for tamarind-braised chicken dishes is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Assam Chicken or tamarind-braised chicken. A0CKM (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor. Dish-level FoodEx2 codes for Southeast Asian preparations are not available in the standard catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Assam Chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of Southeast Asian culinary preparations is absent.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 1 piece of chicken with gravy)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Assam Chicken is approximately 150 g, comprising one bone-in chicken piece with tamarind gravy, as commonly portioned in a Singaporean hawker or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Assam Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Assam Chicken (cr_id F11020001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100g based on HPB chicken dish composition data for similar preparations; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for very low-carbohydrate braised chicken dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Assam Chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. Assam Chicken contains only 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making a formal GI measurement impractical. A GI of 20 (low) is assigned by analogy with other very low-carbohydrate braised chicken preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (20 × 0.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.09, rounded to 0.1, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to ≤100 g per serving, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce, as sodium at 425 mg/100 g poses a meaningful blood-pressure risk despite partial offset from potassium (280 mg/100 g) in tamarind and chicken.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0.3 g per 100 g and sugar 0.1 g per 100 g are negligible; high protein (32.1 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Assam Chicken is an excellent protein choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content (0.3 g/100 g) and high protein (32.1 g/100 g); monitor total meal sodium and avoid pairing with large portions of high-GI rice or sugary accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg/100 g is elevated; saturated fat is moderate at approximately 2.8 g/100 g; high protein and anti-inflammatory spices (tamarind, ginger, garlic) are positive factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit Assam Chicken to a 100–120 g portion to keep sodium intake controlled, choose skinless chicken to reduce saturated fat, and benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in tamarind, ginger, and garlic that may support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, garlic, lemongrass, tamarind, chili/capsaicin); lean white meat with low saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Assam Chicken is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice base; however, individuals sensitive to nightshades (chili, pepper) should monitor personal response, and sodium at 425 mg/100 g should be reduced during preparation to avoid worsening tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Assam Chicken (亚参鸡 / Ayam Asam) is a classic Singaporean and Malaysian Malay dish consisting of chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs or drumsticks — braised or stewed in a tangy tamarind-based gravy. The dish is seasoned with lemongrass, ginger, garlic, shallots, and fresh or dried chili, producing a bold sour-spicy flavour profile with aromatic depth. The texture of the chicken is tender and moist from slow braising, while the gravy is thick, dark, and intensely flavoured. Nutritionally, Assam Chicken is high in protein (32.1 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (10.0 g/100 g), with negligible carbohydrate (0.3 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated at 425 mg/100 g, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The tamarind base provides organic acids and polyphenols with antioxidant properties, while ginger and garlic contribute gingerols and allicin with documented anti-inflammatory effects. Assam Chicken is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres, Malay home kitchens, and Peranakan restaurants, typically accompanied by steamed white rice. The dish is suitable for lunch and dinner and is a staple of Malay and Peranakan cuisine across Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Assam Chicken?
Is Assam Chicken suitable for diabetics?
Is Assam Chicken high in sodium?
What spices are in Assam Chicken?
Can I eat Assam Chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of Assam Chicken?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020001) with confidence 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from HPB comparable chicken dish data. GI assigned by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for very low-carbohydrate braised chicken. All multilingual names verified against standard Malay, Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi culinary terminology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, vitamin, and moisture data for this specific Assam Chicken preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11020001) as of 2026. potassium_mg value of 280 mg/100 g is an estimate based on HPB comparable chicken dish data and tamarind composition; this field is retained as a positive offset for hypertension assessment. Saturated fat of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from HPB chicken dish composition data for similar braised preparations and is not directly reported in the F11020001 entry. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Assam Chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros (protein_g 32.1, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 0.3) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
